---
title: "Lab 4 - The TidyModels Package"
author: student_c
format: html
editor: visual
self-contained: true
reference-location: margin
---

## Introduction

In today's lab, you'll practice building `workflowsets` with `recipes`, `parsnip` models, `rsample` cross validations, model tuning and model comparison.

### Learning goals

By the end of the lab you will...

-   Be able to build workflows to evaluate different models and feature sets.

## Getting started

-   Log in to **your** github account and then go to the [GitHub organization](https://github.com/bsmm-8740-fall-2025) for the course and find the **2025-lab-4-\[your github username\]** repository to complete the lab.

    Create an R project using your **2025-lab-4-\[your github username\]** repository (remember to create a PAT, etc., as in lab-1) and add your answers by editing the `2025-lab-4.qmd` file in your repository.

-   When you are done, be sure to: **save** your document, **stage**, **commit** and [**push**]{.underline} your work.

::: callout-important
To access Github from the lab, you will need to make sure you are logged in as follows:

-   username: **.\\daladmin**
-   password: **Business507!**

Remember to (create a PAT and set your git credentials)

-   create your PAT using `usethis::create_github_token()` ,
-   store your PAT with `gitcreds::gitcreds_set()` ,
-   set your username and email with
    -   `usethis::use_git_config( user.name = ___, user.email = ___)`
:::

## Packages

```{r}
#| message: false
# check if 'librarian' is installed and if not, install it
if (! "librarian" %in% rownames(installed.packages()) ){
  install.packages("librarian")
}
  
# load packages if not already loaded
librarian::shelf(
  tidyverse, magrittr, tidymodels, modeldata, ranger, rsample, broom, recipes, parsnip
)

# set the efault theme for plotting
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 18) + theme(legend.position = "top"))
```

::: callout-warning
## Package installs

If you install packages outside of the ones here, [**remove**]{.underline} your `install.packages(.)` code [**before submitting your solutions**]{.underline}.

Leaving instructions that will install packages may cause your code to fail, but more importantly, it can corrupt the TA/instructor's system.
:::

## The Data

Today we will be using the Ames Housing Data.

This is a data set from [De Cock](http://jse.amstat.org/v19n3/decock.pdf) (2011) has 82 fields were recorded for 2,930 properties in Ames Iowa in the US. The version in the `modeldata` package is copied from the `AmesHousing` package but does not include a few quality columns that appear to be outcomes rather than predictors.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: load ames data
dat <- modeldata::ames
```

The data dictionary can be found on the internet:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: show ames data dictionary
cat(readr::read_file("http://jse.amstat.org/v19n3/decock/DataDocumentation.txt"))
```

## Exercise 1: EDA

Write and execute the code to perform summary EDA on the Ames Housing data using the package `skimr`.

::: {#Q1 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q1:

```{r}
#| label: run EDA on ames data
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(skimr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)


dat <- modeldata::ames


skimr::skim(dat)


na_counts <- vapply(dat, function(x) sum(is.na(x)), integer(1))
na_tbl <- tibble(
  variable     = names(na_counts),
  n_missing    = as.integer(na_counts),
  pct_missing  = round(100 * n_missing / nrow(dat), 2)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_missing))

na_tbl


any_missing <- any(na_counts > 0)
any_missing
```

Is there any missing data? Yes / No - **YES**
:::

## Exercise 2: Train / Test Splits

Write and execute code to create training and test datasets. Have the training dataset represent 75% of the total data.

::: {#Q2 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q2:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: split ames data. into test and training datasets
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK

library(rsample)


dat <- modeldata::ames
stopifnot("Sale_Price" %in% names(dat))  


set.seed(8740)
data_split <- initial_split(dat, prop = 0.75, strata = Sale_Price)

ames_train <- training(data_split)
ames_test  <- testing(data_split)

```
:::

## Exercise 3: Data Preprocessing

create a recipe based on the formula **Sale_Price \~ Longitude + Latitude + Lot_Area + Neighborhood + Year_Sold** with the following steps:

-   transform the variable `Sale_Price` to `log(Sale_Price)`
-   center and scale all predictors
-   create dummy variables for all nominal variables
-   transform the variable `Neighborhood` to pool infrequent values (see `recipes::step_other`)

Think carefully about the order of the steps in your recipe.

Finally prep the recipe.

::: {#Q3 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q3:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: create recipe, add preprocessing steps then prep the recipe. 
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK

#| label: create recipe, add preprocessing steps then prep the recipe. 
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(recipes)

stopifnot(exists("ames_train"))

norm_recipe <-
  recipes::recipe(
    Sale_Price ~ Longitude + Latitude + Lot_Area + Neighborhood + Year_Sold,
    data = ames_train
  ) %>%

  step_log(all_outcomes()) %>%
 
  step_other(Neighborhood, threshold = 0.01, other = "other") %>%

  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  

  step_center(all_predictors()) %>%
  step_scale(all_predictors())

norm_prep <- recipes::prep(norm_recipe)
recipes::tidy(norm_prep)

```
:::

## Exercise 4 Modeling

Create three regression models

-   a base regression model using `lm`

-   a regression model using `glmnet`; set the model parameters `penalty` and `mixture` for tuning

-   a tree model using the `ranger` engine; set the model parameters `min_n` and `trees` for tuning

::: {#Q4 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q4:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: specify lm, elastic net, and random forest, the latter two with with parameter tuning
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK

#| label: specify lm, elastic net, and random forest (tuned)
#| eval: true
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(tidymodels)

# 1) Base regression model using lm
lm_mod_base <-
  linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm")

# (alias kept in case later chunks expect this older name)
m_mod_base <- lm_mod_base

# 2) Elastic net with glmnet (tuned penalty & mixture)
glmnet_mod_tune <-
  linear_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = tune()) |>
  set_engine("glmnet")

# 3) Tree/forest model with ranger (tuned min_n & trees)
rf_mod_tune <-
  rand_forest(min_n = tune(), trees = tune()) |>
  set_engine("ranger") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# (Optional) Show internal code for each model spec
parsnip::translate(lm_mod_base)
parsnip::translate(glmnet_mod_tune)
parsnip::translate(rf_mod_tune)

```
:::

## Exercise 5

Use parsnip::translate() on each model to see the code object that is specific to a particular engine

::: {#Q5 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q5:

```{r}
#| label: show internal code for each model specification, using translate
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK

#| label: show internal code for each model specification, using translate
#| eval: true

library(parsnip)


parsnip::translate(lm_mod_base)
parsnip::translate(glmnet_mod_tune)
parsnip::translate(rf_mod_tune)

```
:::

## Exercise 6 Bootstrap

Create bootstrap samples for the training dataset. You can leave the parameters set to their defaults

::: {#Q6 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q6:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: create bootstrap resamples from the data
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(rsample)

stopifnot(exists("ames_train"))

set.seed(8740)
train_resamples <- ames_train %>% rsample::bootstraps()

train_resamples
```
:::

::: render-commit-push
This is a good place to render, commit, and push changes to your remote lab repo on GitHub. Click the checkbox next to each file in the Git pane to stage the updates you've made, write an informative commit message, and push. After you push the changes, the Git pane in RStudio should be empty.
:::

## Exercise 7

Create workflows with `workflowsets::workflow_set` using your recipe and models.

::: {#Q7 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q7:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: create a workflowset from your recipe and model specifications
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK


#| label: recipe-base
#| eval: true
library(tidymodels)

# make sure training data exists
stopifnot(exists("ames_train"))

# base recipe
rec_base <-
  recipe(Sale_Price ~ ., data = ames_train) |>
  step_log(Sale_Price, base = 10) |>                 # common for Ames
  step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_other(all_nominal_predictors(), threshold = 0.01) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

#| label: create a workflowset from your recipe and model specifications
#| eval: true
library(workflowsets)

stopifnot(
  exists("rec_base"),
  exists("lm_mod_base"),
  exists("glmnet_mod_tune"),
  exists("rf_mod_tune")
)

all_workflows <-
  workflow_set(
    preproc = list(base = rec_base),
    models  = list(
      lm      = lm_mod_base,
      glmnet  = glmnet_mod_tune,
      ranger  = rf_mod_tune
    ),
    cross = TRUE
  )

all_workflows


```

```{r}
#| label: unnest the info column of all_workflows to show the workflow structure 
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK



library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)


all_workflows %>%
  tidyr::unnest_wider(info)








```
:::

## Exercise 8

Map the default function (`tune::tune_grid()`) across the workflows in the workflowset you just created and update the variable `all_workflows` with the result.

This will take some time to complete.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: map tune_grid across all workflows

# This may take time


library(workflowsets)
library(tune)

stopifnot(exists("all_workflows"), exists("train_resamples"))

set.seed(8740)
all_workflows <- all_workflows %>% 
  workflow_map(
    fn        = "tune_grid",                 # call tune_grid
    resamples = train_resamples,             # e.g., vfold_cv(ames_train, v = 5)
    grid      = 5,
    control   = control_grid(verbose = TRUE, save_pred = FALSE)
  )

```

The updated variable `all_workflows` contains a nested column named **result**, and each cell of the column **result** is a tibble containing a nested column named **.metrics**. Write code to

1.  un-nest the metrics in the column .metrics

2.  filter out the rows for the metric rmse

3.  group by wflow_id, order the .estimate column from highest to lowest, and pick out the first row of each group

::: {#Q8 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q8:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: unnest metrics, filter for rmse, group by workflow id and order metric in descending order
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK

#| label: unnest-metrics-filter-rmse-pick-top
#| eval: true
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)

# Collect metrics from each tuned result
metrics_long <- all_workflows %>%
  mutate(metrics_tbl = map(result, ~ tune::collect_metrics(.x))) %>%
  select(wflow_id, metrics_tbl) %>%
  unnest(metrics_tbl) %>%
  filter(.metric == "rmse")

# Ensure a `.estimate` column exists (older/newer tidymodels use `mean`)
if (!(".estimate" %in% names(metrics_long)) && ("mean" %in% names(metrics_long))) {
  metrics_long <- metrics_long %>% mutate(.estimate = mean)
}

# Group by workflow, order .estimate HIGH→LOW (as requested), pick top row per group
rmse_top <- metrics_long %>%
  group_by(wflow_id) %>%
  arrange(desc(.estimate), .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup()

rmse_top

```

Look at the columns to the right of the .config column (columns 7,8,9,10) to see the tuned parameters for each model.
:::

## Exercise 9

Run the code below and compare to your results from exercise 8.

::: {#Q9 .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q9:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: compare output of rank_results to manual calculation in Q8
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK

#| label: compare output of rank_results to manual calculation in Q8
#| eval: true
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(workflowsets)
library(dplyr)

# 1) Use rank_results (tidymodels helper)
rr <- workflowsets::rank_results(all_workflows, rank_metric = "rmse")

# Depending on tidymodels version, the metric column may be `mean` or `.estimate`
if ("mean" %in% names(rr)) {
  rr <- rr %>% mutate(score = mean)
} else if (".estimate" %in% names(rr)) {
  rr <- rr %>% mutate(score = .estimate)
} else {
  stop("Neither `mean` nor `.estimate` is present in rank_results output.")
}

rr_top <- rr %>%
  filter(.metric == "rmse") %>%
  group_by(wflow_id) %>%
  arrange(desc(score), .by_group = TRUE) %>%   # highest → lowest, per Exercise 8 instructions
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(wflow_id, rank_results_score = score, rank)

# 2) Bring in manual results from Exercise 8
# (rmse_top should already exist; it has wflow_id + manual_score/.estimate)
if (!exists("rmse_top")) stop("Run Exercise 8 first to create `rmse_top`!")

manual <- rmse_top %>%
  rename(manual_score = .estimate)

# 3) Compare side-by-side
comparison <- rr_top %>%
  left_join(manual, by = "wflow_id") %>%
  mutate(delta = rank_results_score - manual_score)

comparison





```
:::

## Exercise 10

Select the best model per the **rsme** metric using its id.

::: {#Q10a .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q10a:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: select the est model by the rmse metric
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK


#| label: select the best model by the rmse metric
#| eval: true
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(dplyr)

# Collect all metrics from the tuned workflow set
all_metrics <- all_workflows %>%
  workflowsets::collect_metrics()

# If your version returns `mean` instead of `.estimate`, normalize it
if ("mean" %in% names(all_metrics)) {
  all_metrics <- all_metrics %>% mutate(.estimate = mean)
}

# Filter only rmse
rmse_results <- all_metrics %>%
  filter(.metric == "rmse")

# --- Option A (usual practice): pick the *lowest* RMSE (best model) ---
best_rmse <- rmse_results %>%
  group_by(wflow_id) %>%
  slice_min(order_by = .estimate, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(.estimate) %>%
  slice(1)

best_rmse

# --- Option B (if your exercise still wants "highest → lowest") ---
worst_rmse <- rmse_results %>%
  group_by(wflow_id) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = .estimate, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(desc(.estimate)) %>%
  slice(1)

worst_rmse

```
:::

Finalize the workflow by setting the parameters for the best model

::: {#Q10b .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q10b:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: use finalize_workflow with parameters of best fit to identify best model
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK


library(workflowsets)
library(workflows)
library(tune)

# 1) Identify which workflow ID had the best RMSE
best_result <- workflowsets::rank_results(all_workflows, rank_metric = "rmse") %>%
  filter(.metric == "rmse") %>%
  arrange(mean) %>%          # lowest RMSE = best
  slice(1)

best_result$wflow_id


# 2) Extract the tune_result object for that workflow
best_tune <- workflowsets::extract_workflow_set_result(
  all_workflows,
  id = best_result$wflow_id
)

# 3) Select the best set of parameters from that result
best_params <- select_best(best_tune, metric = "rmse")
best_params

# 4) Extract the actual workflow (recipe + model spec)
best_wflow <- workflowsets::extract_workflow(
  all_workflows,
  id = best_result$wflow_id
)

# 5) Finalize the workflow with those parameters
final_wflow <- finalize_workflow(best_wflow, best_params)

final_wflow


```
:::

Now compare the fits

library(dplyr)

library(recipes)

library(workflows)

library(parsnip)

library(yardstick)

library(rsample)

if (!exists("ames_train") \|\| !exists("ames_test")) {

dat \<- modeldata::ames

set.seed(8740)

data_split \<- initial_split(dat, prop = 0.75, strata = Sale_Price)

ames_train \<- training(data_split)

ames_test \<- testing(data_split)

}

norm_recipe2 \<-

recipe(

Sale_Price \~ Longitude + Latitude + Lot_Area + Neighborhood + Year_Sold,

data = ames_train

) %\>%

step_log(all_outcomes(), skip = TRUE) %\>%

step_other(Neighborhood, threshold = 0.01, other = "other") %\>%

step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %\>%

step_center(all_predictors()) %\>%

step_scale(all_predictors())

wf \<- if (exists("best_model_workflow")) {

best_model_workflow %\>% workflows::update_recipe(norm_recipe2)

} else {

workflows::workflow() %\>%

add_model(parsnip::linear_reg() %\>% set_engine("lm")) %\>%

add_recipe(norm_recipe2)

}

final_fit \<- fit(wf, data = ames_train)

train_pred \<- predict(final_fit, new_data = ames_train) %\>%

bind_cols(ames_train %\>% select(Sale_Price)) %\>%

mutate(truth_log = log(Sale_Price))

test_pred \<- predict(final_fit, new_data = ames_test) %\>%

bind_cols(ames_test %\>% select(Sale_Price)) %\>%

mutate(truth_log = log(Sale_Price))

mse_train \<- yardstick::mse(train_pred, truth = truth_log, estimate = .pred)\$.estimate

mse_test \<- yardstick::mse(test_pred, truth = truth_log, estimate = .pred)\$.estimate

mse_ratio \<- mse_test / mse_train

mse_ratio

::: {#Q10c .callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## YOUR ANSWER Q10c:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| label: fit the best model to train and test data and compare predictions
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK


#| label: fit the best model to train and test data and compare predictions
#| eval: true
# PLEASE SHOW YOUR WORK
library(dplyr)
library(recipes)
library(workflows)
library(parsnip)
library(yardstick)
library(rsample)

# -----------------------------
# 1) Data split (if not already created)
# -----------------------------
if (!exists("ames_train") || !exists("ames_test")) {
  dat <- modeldata::ames
  set.seed(8740)
  data_split <- initial_split(dat, prop = 0.75, strata = Sale_Price)
  ames_train <- training(data_split)
  ames_test  <- testing(data_split)
}

# -----------------------------
# 2) Recipe
# -----------------------------
norm_recipe2 <-
  recipe(
    Sale_Price ~ Longitude + Latitude + Lot_Area + Neighborhood + Year_Sold,
    data = ames_train
  ) %>%
  step_log(all_outcomes(), skip = TRUE) %>%
  step_other(Neighborhood, threshold = 0.01, other = "other") %>%
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_center(all_predictors()) %>%
  step_scale(all_predictors())

# -----------------------------
# 3) Workflow (use best model if exists, else simple lm)
# -----------------------------
wf <- if (exists("final_wflow")) {
  final_wflow %>% update_recipe(norm_recipe2)
} else {
  workflow() %>%
    add_model(linear_reg() %>% set_engine("lm")) %>%
    add_recipe(norm_recipe2)
}

# -----------------------------
# 4) Fit and predict
# -----------------------------
final_fit <- fit(wf, data = ames_train)

train_pred <- predict(final_fit, new_data = ames_train) %>%
  bind_cols(ames_train %>% select(Sale_Price)) %>%
  mutate(truth_log = log(Sale_Price))

test_pred <- predict(final_fit, new_data = ames_test) %>%
  bind_cols(ames_test %>% select(Sale_Price)) %>%
  mutate(truth_log = log(Sale_Price))

# -----------------------------
# 5) Compute MSE manually
# -----------------------------
mse_train <- mean((train_pred$.pred - train_pred$truth_log)^2)
mse_test  <- mean((test_pred$.pred  - test_pred$truth_log)^2)

mse_ratio <- mse_test / mse_train
mse_ratio









```

What is the ratio of the OOB prediction errors (MSE): test/train? \_\_

```         
1.003876
```

\_\_

```         
```
:::

::: render-commit-push
You're done and ready to submit your work! **Save**, **stage**, **commit**, and **push** all remaining changes. You can use the commit message "Done with Lab 4!", and make sure you have committed and pushed all changed files to GitHub (your Git pane in RStudio should be empty) and that **all** documents are updated in your repo on GitHub.
:::

::: callout-warning
## Submission

I will pull (copy) everyone's submissions at 5:00pm on the Sunday following class, and I will work only with these copies, so anything submitted after 5:00pm will not be graded. (**don't forget to commit and then push your work by 5:00pm on Sunday!**)
:::

## Grading

Total points available: 30 points.

![](images/rubric10.png){fig-align="center" width="600"}
